summary:
The Greek War of Independence, fought from 1821 to 1829, was a successful uprising of Greek revolutionaries against Ottoman rule that established the first independent Greek state since the ancient and Byzantine periods. The conflict attracted enormous international attention — it was seen by educated Europeans and Americans as the struggle of the cradle of Western civilization against Islamic despotism — and became a cause célèbre for the Romantic movement.

The revolution was sparked by the uprising of Bishop Germanos at Patras in March 1821 and rapidly spread across the Greek mainland and islands. Greek forces achieved early successes, capturing significant territory and declaring independence at the First National Assembly of Epidaurus in January 1822. However, the revolution descended into factional infighting and was nearly crushed when the Egyptian forces of Muhammad Ali, acting for the Ottomans, intervened with a modern army and navy that devastated the Peloponnese and besieged Missolonghi — where the poet Lord Byron died in 1824, becoming a martyred symbol of the philhellenic cause.

The decisive turning point came through great-power intervention. Britain, France, and Russia — motivated by strategic interests in the Eastern Mediterranean as well as public sympathy for the Greeks — intervened militarily. At the Battle of Navarino in October 1827, a combined British-French-Russian fleet destroyed the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet in the last great battle of the age of sail. Russia then declared war on the Ottoman Empire directly in 1828, and under the pressure of military defeat the Ottomans accepted Greek autonomy in the Treaty of London and then full independence in the Treaty of Constantinople in 1832.

The new Greek state was tiny compared to the areas of Greek cultural and historical presence — the 'Great Idea' of unifying all Greeks into one nation would drive Greek foreign policy for a century. Nevertheless, the establishment of an independent Greece was historically significant, representing the first successful challenge to Ottoman territorial integrity in Europe and inspiring nationalist movements across the Balkans and beyond.
